region_of_origin: Italy (espresso tradition); served globally via Starbucks

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 covers latte as a general category. No specific code exists for double-strength or double-shot latte variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers coffee with milk broadly. No specific facet code for double-strength espresso ratio; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 covers latte as a substance. Double-strength variant not separately coded; closest_parent used.

serving.description: 1 cup (approximately 355 ml / 12 fl oz tall size)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tall-size cup (approximately 355 g) of Double Strength Caffè Latte, comprising two shots of espresso topped with steamed whole milk.

nutrient_source.title: Starbucks Caffè Latte with Whole Milk — Nutrition Information (Tall, 12 fl oz); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central: Beverages, coffee, latte, with whole milk (FDC ID 174878)
nutrient_source.organization: Starbucks Corporation Nutrition Information / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: A standard Starbucks Caffè Latte (tall, whole milk) contains approximately 1 shot of espresso. The Double Strength variant uses 2 shots with a lesser milk ratio. Nutrient values are adapted from the standard latte profile (USDA FDC 174878 and Starbucks published nutrition) with adjustment for the higher espresso-to-milk ratio: slightly higher caffeine and marginally lower carbohydrate and fat per 100 g due to reduced milk proportion. Per-100g values are derived by dividing the per-serving values by the serving weight (355 g). Values represent a reasonable estimate; exact Starbucks double-strength formulation is not separately published in an approved primary nutrient database.

gi_source.title: Milk, full fat — GI value 27 (±4); Latte analogous estimate based on whole-milk GI and low espresso carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a double-strength caffè latte. The GI is estimated at 30 based on the dominant glycemic contributor being whole milk (GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database). The espresso component contributes negligible carbohydrate. A small upward adjustment to 30 accounts for the lactose in steamed whole milk and minor variability. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 16.0) / 100 ≈ 4.8; rounded to 6.1 using the adapted per-100g carbohydrate of 4.5 g × 3.55 serving factor = 15.975 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = (30 × 15.975) / 100 = 4.79 ≈ 4.8. Value reported as 6.1 is corrected: GL = (30 × 4.5 × 355/100) / 100 = (30 × 15.975) / 100 = 4.79. Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving = 4.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~42 mg per 100 g; ~149 mg per serving) and caffeine-related transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension may consume a double-strength caffè latte in moderation, noting that the sodium content per serving is modest (~149 mg), but the double espresso dose (~150 mg caffeine per serving) may cause a transient blood pressure rise and should be limited to 1–2 cups per day per clinical guidance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Lactose-derived sugar (~14.9 g per serving) and moderate glycemic index (~30) from whole milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should account for the approximately 15 g of naturally occurring lactose-derived sugar per serving when calculating carbohydrate intake; switching to a reduced-fat or plant-based milk alternative and limiting added syrups can lower the glycemic impact of this beverage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whole milk (~3.9 g per serving) and cholesterol (~24.9 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat contribution from whole milk (~3.9 g per serving) is moderate; substituting with low-fat or plant-based milk reduces saturated fat intake, and the beverage can be included as part of a heart-healthy diet when consumed without added syrups or whipped cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Strength Caffè Latte is a coffee-based milk beverage made with two shots of espresso combined with a lesser proportion of perfectly steamed whole milk compared to a standard caffè latte. The result is a richer, bolder, and more intensely coffee-flavoured drink with a creamy texture derived from the steamed milk. At Starbucks Singapore, this drink is prepared using their signature espresso blend, which produces a robust, slightly bitter espresso base that is balanced by the natural sweetness and creaminess of whole milk. The beverage is served hot and is popular among coffee enthusiasts who prefer a stronger espresso character without the full intensity of a straight espresso or Americano. Nutritionally, a tall serving (approximately 355 ml) provides around 156 kcal, 8.9 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 16 g carbohydrate, predominantly from lactose in the milk. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), making it a relatively moderate glycemic beverage. The double espresso dose delivers approximately 150 mg of caffeine per serving, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing blood pressure, anxiety disorders, or caffeine sensitivity. Calcium content is notable (~391 mg per serving), contributing meaningfully to daily calcium requirements. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor the lactose-derived sugar content, and those with cardiovascular disease should consider substituting whole milk with a lower-fat alternative.

confidence_reason: The Double Strength Caffè Latte is a Starbucks-specific menu variant not separately listed in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are adapted from the standard Starbucks Caffè Latte (whole milk, tall) published nutrition data and USDA FDC entry for latte with whole milk (FDC ID 174878), adjusted for the higher espresso-to-milk ratio. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is defensible and based on approved secondary sources, but exact formulation data for the double-strength variant is not independently verified in a primary approved database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FDC ID 174878 (Beverages, coffee, latte, with whole milk) and Starbucks published nutrition for a standard tall latte, adjusted for the double-espresso, reduced-milk ratio of the double-strength variant. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: coffee beverages with milk contain no dietary fibre per USDA FDC data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC source for this beverage category. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: neither espresso nor whole milk contributes meaningful vitamin C. 5. glycemic_index set to 30 (analogous estimate based on whole milk GI ~27 from University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category is low. Glycemic load per serving corrected to 4.8: GL = (30 × 15.975 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 4.79 ≈ 4.8. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC 174878 and are not -1 as the source provides reasonable analogous values. 7. This is a Coffee-cuisine item served globally; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, etc.) are not applicable as this is an internationally branded English-named product with no established local-language canonical names. The aliases_by_language object captures English variants only. 8. Starbucks Singapore field survey listing confirms the product is served at Starbucks One Fullerton at SGD 6.70. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology code exists for double-strength latte variant.
